binaries.title: ltotape.sys - LTO-9 density code (0x60) support added to GetMediaTypes callback

changed_functions.name: GetMediaTypes
key_changes: Adds acceptance of SCSI density code 0x60 to the media-type path. Unpatched accepts density codes {0x00,0x40,0x42,0x44,0x46,0x58,0x5A,0x5C,0x5E} via a single range check (density-0x40 <= 0x1E) with bitmask 0x55000055; patched accepts exactly that same set plus 0x60 via a two-branch test (density <= 0x58 with bitmask 0x1000055, and density > 0x58 with explicit checks for 0x5A/0x5C/0x5E/0x60). The accepted set only grows by the single value 0x60.

Not a pointer bug: unpatched reads [rdi+0x18] and patched reads [r9+0x18], but rdi is set to r9 in the prologue (mov rdi, r9 at 0x1C0001CC5), so both read the same a4->DataBuffer. Both decompilations render this as a4->DataBuffer.

Not a hardcoded-mode bug: both builds store 0x1E (decimal 30) on the a6==30 && a5==1 branch (*a2 = 30). Unpatched sources 0x1E from scratch register r9d (loaded at 0x1C0001CF2); patched uses the immediate/ecx which equals 0x1E on that verified path. Same stored value; mov [r14], r9d at 0x1C0001D13 becomes mov [r14], ecx at 0x1C0001D0E.

Register-allocation churn: density code held in bpl (was r12b); 24-bit capacity value held in r12d (was ebp). The output write block (mov [r15+0x11], density; mov [r15+0x8], capacity) is otherwise identical between builds.
changed_functions.security_notes: No security-relevant change. GetMediaTypes populates density and capacity fields of the returned media descriptors for recognized density codes read from byte offset 4 of the sense data buffer (a4->DataBuffer). The only functional difference is that the patched build additionally accepts density code 0x60 (LTO-9); the previously accepted set is unchanged and nothing was removed or tightened, so the patched build is marginally less restrictive, not more. This is not a memory-safety condition: when a density code is accepted, the density byte is written to [r15+0x11] and the 24-bit capacity value to [r15+0x8] identically in both builds, and the descriptor loop is bounded by the fixed-size LTOMedia table. Accepting one more density value changes no bound and adds no out-of-range access. The absence of the 0x60 accept path in the unpatched build simply means an LTO-9 device gets a media descriptor without its density/capacity fields filled in; there is no crash, over-read, or corruption.
changed_functions.assembly_diff:
UNPATCHED density accept test (0x1C0001DC1-0x1C0001E05):
0x1C0001DC1: mov   rcx, [rdi+18h]     ; rdi == r9 -> a4->DataBuffer
0x1C0001DCD: mov   r12b, [rcx+4]      ; density code
0x1C0001DE7: test  r12b, r12b
0x1C0001DEA: jz    0x1C0001E00        ; density 0 accepted
0x1C0001DEC: lea   eax, [r12-40h]
0x1C0001DF1: cmp   al, r9b            ; r9b = 0x1E
0x1C0001DF4: ja    0x1C0001E1C        ; reject if (density-0x40) > 0x1E
0x1C0001DF6: mov   ecx, 55000055h
0x1C0001DFB: bt    ecx, eax
0x1C0001DFE: jnb   0x1C0001E1C        ; reject if bit clear
0x1C0001E00: mov   ebx, 56h           ; accept
0x1C0001E05: jmp   0x1C0001E1C

PATCHED density accept test (0x1C0001DBC-0x1C0001E1B):
0x1C0001DBC: mov   rcx, [r9+18h]      ; a4->DataBuffer (same location)
0x1C0001DC9: mov   bpl, [rcx+4]       ; density code
0x1C0001DE7: cmp   bpl, 58h
0x1C0001DEB: ja    0x1C0001E03        ; density > 0x58 -> new branch
0x1C0001DED: lea   eax, [rbp-40h]
0x1C0001DF0: cmp   al, 18h
0x1C0001DF2: ja    0x1C0001DFE
0x1C0001DF4: mov   ecx, 1000055h
0x1C0001DF9: bt    ecx, eax
0x1C0001DFC: jb    0x1C0001E16        ; accept 0x40,0x42,0x44,0x46,0x58
0x1C0001DFE: test  bpl, bpl
0x1C0001E01: jmp   0x1C0001E14        ; density 0 special case
0x1C0001E03: lea   eax, [rbp-5Ah]
0x1C0001E06: test  al, 0F9h
0x1C0001E08: jnz   0x1C0001E10
0x1C0001E0A: cmp   bpl, 60h
0x1C0001E0E: jnz   0x1C0001E16        ; 0x5A/0x5C/0x5E accept
0x1C0001E10: cmp   bpl, 60h
0x1C0001E14: jnz   0x1C0001E33        ; reject
0x1C0001E16: mov   ebx, 56h           ; accept (now reached by 0x60 too)
0x1C0001E1B: jmp   0x1C0001E33

security_findings.entry_point: GetMediaTypes tape class callback (reached on the media-type query path with a5==2, a6==3, *a2==0)
call_chain: DriverEntry (0x1C0001008) registers the tape callbacks via TapeClassInitialize
Tape class library dispatches the media-type query
GetMediaTypes (0x1C0001CA0) parses sense data and fills media descriptors
security_findings.description: The patch adds acceptance of SCSI density code 0x60 (LTO-9) to GetMediaTypes. The unpatched build accepts density codes {0x00,0x40,0x42,0x44,0x46,0x58,0x5A,0x5C,0x5E}; the patched build accepts that same set plus 0x60. No accepted code was removed and no check was tightened, so this is a relaxation (new-generation media support), not a security fix. When a density code is accepted, the density byte and 24-bit capacity value are written to fixed output-descriptor fields ([r15+0x11] and [r15+0x8]) identically in both builds; the descriptor loop is bounded by the fixed-size LTOMedia table. On the unpatched build a device reporting 0x60 simply receives a media descriptor with those two fields left unpopulated. The previously reported 'wrong SRB pointer' and 'hardcoded 0x1E mode' items are not defects: rdi==r9 (mov rdi, r9 at 0x1C0001CC5) so [rdi+0x18] and [r9+0x18] are the same read, and both builds store 0x1E on the a6==30 branch.
security_findings.trigger_conditions: A tape device (real LTO-9 drive or emulated SCSI sequential-access device) reports density code 0x60 at byte offset 4 of the sense data buffer, and an application queries tape media types so GetMediaTypes runs with a5==2, a6==3, *a2==0. The only difference between builds is whether the returned 0x60 descriptor has its density/capacity fields populated.
security_findings.primitive: None. The change removes no validation and adds no attacker-reachable memory-safety condition. Every write on the accept path is a fixed-size store to a bounded output-descriptor field; the destination and size are independent of the density value.
security_findings.assembly:
UNPATCHED density accept test:
0x1C0001DC1: mov   rcx, [rdi+18h]     ; rdi == r9 -> a4->DataBuffer
0x1C0001DC5: movzx eax, byte [rcx+0Ah]
0x1C0001DC9: movzx ebp, byte [rcx+9]
0x1C0001DCD: mov   r12b, byte [rcx+4] ; density code
0x1C0001DD1: mov   r13b, byte [rcx+1]
0x1C0001DD5: mov   r14b, byte [rcx+2]
0x1C0001DD9: shl   ebp, 8
0x1C0001DDC: or    ebp, eax
0x1C0001DDE: movzx eax, byte [rcx+0Bh]
0x1C0001DE2: shl   ebp, 8
0x1C0001DE5: or    ebp, eax
0x1C0001DE7: test  r12b, r12b
0x1C0001DEA: jz    0x1C0001E00        ; density 0 accepted
0x1C0001DEC: lea   eax, [r12-40h]
0x1C0001DF1: cmp   al, r9b            ; r9b = 0x1E
0x1C0001DF4: ja    0x1C0001E1C        ; reject if > 0x1E (0x60: 0x20 > 0x1E)
0x1C0001DF6: mov   ecx, 55000055h
0x1C0001DFB: bt    ecx, eax
0x1C0001DFE: jnb   0x1C0001E1C        ; reject if bit clear
0x1C0001E00: mov   ebx, 56h           ; accept
0x1C0001E05: jmp   0x1C0001E1C

PATCHED density accept test:
0x1C0001DBC: mov   rcx, [r9+18h]      ; a4->DataBuffer (same location)
0x1C0001DC0: movzx eax, byte [rcx+0Ah]
0x1C0001DC4: movzx r12d, byte [rcx+9]
0x1C0001DC9: mov   bpl, byte [rcx+4]  ; density code
0x1C0001DCD: mov   r13b, byte [rcx+1]
0x1C0001DD1: mov   r14b, byte [rcx+2]
0x1C0001DD5: shl   r12d, 8
0x1C0001DD9: or    r12d, eax
0x1C0001DDC: movzx eax, byte [rcx+0Bh]
0x1C0001DE0: shl   r12d, 8
0x1C0001DE4: or    r12d, eax
0x1C0001DE7: cmp   bpl, 58h
0x1C0001DEB: ja    0x1C0001E03
0x1C0001DED: lea   eax, [rbp-40h]
0x1C0001DF0: cmp   al, 18h
0x1C0001DF2: ja    0x1C0001DFE
0x1C0001DF4: mov   ecx, 1000055h
0x1C0001DF9: bt    ecx, eax
0x1C0001DFC: jb    0x1C0001E16        ; accept 0x40,0x42,0x44,0x46,0x58
0x1C0001DFE: test  bpl, bpl
0x1C0001E01: jmp   0x1C0001E14
0x1C0001E03: lea   eax, [rbp-5Ah]
0x1C0001E06: test  al, 0F9h
0x1C0001E08: jnz   0x1C0001E10
0x1C0001E0A: cmp   bpl, 60h
0x1C0001E0E: jnz   0x1C0001E16        ; 0x5A/0x5C/0x5E accept
0x1C0001E10: cmp   bpl, 60h
0x1C0001E14: jnz   0x1C0001E33        ; reject
0x1C0001E16: mov   ebx, 56h           ; accept (now reached by 0x60 too)
0x1C0001E1B: jmp   0x1C0001E33

Output write block (identical effect in both builds):
mov   [r15+11h], r12b (unpatched) / bpl (patched)   ; density code byte
mov   [r15+8],   ebp  (unpatched) / r12d (patched)  ; 24-bit capacity value
security_findings.notes: The only functional difference between the two builds is the addition of density code 0x60 (LTO-9) to the accepted set. No fault is reproducible: the difference is which fields of a returned media descriptor are filled in. ltotape.sys is a tape minidriver that imports TapeClassAllocateSrbBuffer, TapeClassInitialize, and TapeClassZeroMemory. The LTOMedia table at 0x1C0003110 holds the media identifiers iterated by the descriptor loop. Image base 0x1C0000000.

summary: The patch modifies a single function, the tape minidriver's GetMediaTypes callback (0x1C0001CA0), to add acceptance of SCSI density code 0x60 (LTO-9). Enumerating the accepted density codes for both builds shows the patched set equals the unpatched set {0x00,0x40,0x42,0x44,0x46,0x58,0x5A,0x5C,0x5E} plus 0x60; nothing is removed or tightened. This is a functional feature addition (new tape generation support), not a security fix. The density/capacity write path is identical and bounded in both builds. The previously reported 'wrong SRB pointer' (rdi==r9 via mov rdi, r9 at 0x1C0001CC5) and 'hardcoded 0x1E mode' (both builds store 0x1E on the a6==30 branch) are not defects. No other function changed; all others are identical apart from relocation.
